---
title: "WGCNA for leaf in control, consensus test for four lines. Part II - network construction."
format: html
date: "2026-04-23"
execute:
  fig-format: png
  fig.dpi: 300
---

Built with R version `r getRversion()`

This script continues [02-MAD-wgcna-leaf-ctrl-line4-1data.qmd](02-MAD-wgcna-leaf-ctrl-line4-1data.qmd).

# Data preparation

## Environment cleaning and set-up

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
# load required packages
#rm(list = ls())
library(WGCNA)

# The following setting is important, do not omit.
options(stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
# Allow multi-threading within WGCNA.
enableWGCNAThreads()
```

:::{.callout-important}
Using MAD-filtered data (above 1st quartile).
:::

```{r load}
rm(list = ls())
# Load the data saved in the first part
lnames = load(file = "../rdata-saved/wgcna/Consensus-dataInput-MAD025.RData")
#The variable lnames contains the names of loaded variables.
lnames
# Get the number of sets in the multiExpr structure.
nSets = checkSets(multiExpr)$nSets
```

## Step-by-step network construction and module detection

### Choosing the soft-thresholding power: analysis of network topology

The three different network types are coded and quoted in `pickSoftThreshold` function.
Likewise, there is `bicor` version.

:::{.callout-important}
According to FAQ:
> We strongly recommend using the argument maxPOutliers = 0.05 or 0.10 whenever the biweight midcorrelation is used
:::

```{r thresholding powers, cache=TRUE}
# Choose a set of soft-thresholding powers
# powers = c(seq(10, 30, by=1))
powers = c(seq(5, 15, by=1))
# Initialize a list to hold the results of scale-free analysis
powerTables = vector(mode = "list", length = nSets)

# Call the network topology analysis function for each set in turn
for (set in 1:nSets)
powerTables[[set]] = list(data = pickSoftThreshold(multiExpr[[set]]$data, 
                                                   powerVector=powers,
                                                   verbose = 2, 
                                                   networkType = "signed hybrid", 
                                                   corFnc = 'bicor', 
                                                   corOptions = list(maxPOutliers = 0.05),
                                                   moreNetworkConcepts = TRUE)[[2]])
# additional measures ("moreNetworkConcepts") described in https://journals.plos.org/ploscompbiol/article?id=10.1371/journal.pcbi.1000117
# Density : close to 1, all genes are strongly correlated, ~ 0.5 more ambigous correlations
# Centralization: close to 1, one gene strongly correlated with all others which in turn have negative correlations
# Heterogeneity: can be used to measure variation of connectivity of genes.

# Optionally
# networkType = "signed", corFnc = 'bicor'
# networkType = "signed hybrid", corFnc = 'bicor'

collectGarbage()
```

```{r plot thresholds, fig.width=8, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
# Plot the results:
colors = c("black", "red", 'blue', 'violet')
# Will plot these columns of the returned scale free analysis tables
plotCols = c(2,5,6,7)
colNames = c("Scale Free Topology Model Fit", "Mean connectivity", "Median connectivity",
"Max connectivity")

# Get the minima and maxima of the plotted points
ylim = matrix(NA, nrow = 2, ncol = 4);
for (set in 1:nSets)
{
for (col in 1:length(plotCols))
{
ylim[1, col] = min(ylim[1, col], powerTables[[set]]$data[, plotCols[col]], na.rm = TRUE)
ylim[2, col] = max(ylim[2, col], powerTables[[set]]$data[, plotCols[col]], na.rm = TRUE)
}
}

# Plot the quantities in the chosen columns vs. the soft thresholding power
# sizeGrWindow(8, 6) # not used here
par(mfcol = c(2,2))
par(mar = c(4.2, 4.2 , 2.2, 0.5))
cex1 = 0.7;
for (col in 1:length(plotCols)) for (set in 1:nSets)
{
if (set==1)
{
plot(powerTables[[set]]$data[,1], -sign(powerTables[[set]]$data[,3])*powerTables[[set]]$data[,2],
xlab="Soft Threshold (power)",ylab=colNames[col],type="n", ylim = ylim[, col],
main = colNames[col]);
addGrid();
}
if (col==1)
{
text(powerTables[[set]]$data[,1], -sign(powerTables[[set]]$data[,3])*powerTables[[set]]$data[,2],
labels=powers,cex=cex1,col=colors[set])
} else
text(powerTables[[set]]$data[,1], powerTables[[set]]$data[,plotCols[col]],
labels=powers,cex=cex1,col=colors[set])
if (col==1)
{
legend("bottomright", legend = setLabels, col = colors, pch = 20) ;
} else
legend("topright", legend = setLabels, col = colors, pch = 20) ;
}
```

:::{.callout-important}
From [thread](https://support.bioconductor.org/p/121622/#121653).
Peter Langfelder (the package developer) regarding "mean connectivity" advises:
> I aim for mean connectivity around 100 or less (typically between 30-50); median connectivity usually ends up around 10.

Also, the FAQ states that for "signed hybrid" power should by < 15.
Using "signed hybrid", potentially power 12 or 13 is good, based on "SFT.R.sq", "mean.k" and "median.k", averaged across samples.

:::

```{r save-intermediate}
save.image('../rdata-saved/wgcna/leaf-before-adjacencies.RDa')
```

<!-- ################ START of code executed externally ########################## -->


### Calculation of network adjacencies

:::{.callout-note}
As they are computationally demanding the adjacencies and TOMsimilarity calculations were moved to external R file for batch running [TOMs_powers.R](TOMs_powers.R).
:::

### Calculation of topological overlap

:::{.callout-important}
`TOMtype` is different than `networkType`, so using `networkType = "signed"` does not imply using `TOMtype = "signed"`!!! [post](https://support.bioconductor.org/p/73814/#73894)
:::

<!-- ################ END of code executed externally ########################## -->

### Scaling of Topological Overlap Matrices to make them comparable across sets

Topological Overlap Matrices of different data sets may have different statistical properties. For example, the
TOM in the one data may be systematically lower than the TOM in the other. Since consensus is defined
as the component-wise minimum of the all TOMs, a bias may result. Here we illustrate a simple scaling that
mitigates the effect of different statistical properties to some degree. We scale the all TOMs such that the 95th
percentile equals the 95th percentile of one of the TOMs (a554)

```{r load2}
rm(list=ls())
load('../rdata-saved/wgcna/leaf-before-adjacencies.RDa')
load('../rdata-saved/wgcna/power13.RDa')
```

```{r scale TOM, cache=TRUE}
# Define the reference percentile
scaleP = 0.95
set.seed(1)
# Sample sufficiently large number of TOM entries
nSamplesTOM = as.integer(1/(1-scaleP) * 1000)
# Choose the sampled TOM entries
scaleSample = sample(nGenes*(nGenes-1)/2, size = nSamplesTOM)
TOMScalingSamples = list()
# These are TOM values at reference percentile
scaleQuant = rep(1, nSets)
# Scaling powers to equalize reference TOM values
scalePowers = rep(1, nSets)

# Loop over sets
for (set in 1:nSets)
{
  # Select the sampled TOM entries
  TOMScalingSamples[[set]] = as.dist(TOM[set, , ])[scaleSample]
  # Calculate the 95th percentile
  scaleQuant[set] = quantile(TOMScalingSamples[[set]],
                             probs = scaleP, 
                             type = 8)
  # Scale the TOMs to the first
  
  if (set>1)
    {
    scalePowers[set] = log(scaleQuant[1])/log(scaleQuant[set])
    TOM[set, ,] = TOM[set, ,]^scalePowers[set]
    }
}
```

The array `TOM` now contains the scaled TOMs. To see what the scaling achieved, we form a pairwise quantile-quantile plots
of the topological overlaps before and after scaling

```{r qqplot TOM, fig.width=6, fig.height=6, dpi=600, dev='png'}
par(mfrow = c(3, 2), mar = c(4,4,2,1))  # 2 wiersze x 3 kolumny

# For plotting, also scale the sampled TOM entries
scaledTOMSamples = list();
for (set in 1:nSets)
  scaledTOMSamples[[set]] = TOMScalingSamples[[set]]^scalePowers[set]
# Open a suitably sized graphics window
# sizeGrWindow(6,6)
# qq plot of the unscaled samples
qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[1]], TOMScalingSamples[[2]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
  xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[1]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[2]),
  main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[1]], scaledTOMSamples[[2]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[1]], TOMScalingSamples[[3]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[1]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[3]),
main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[1]], scaledTOMSamples[[3]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[1]], TOMScalingSamples[[4]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
  xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[1]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[4]),
  main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[1]], scaledTOMSamples[[4]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[2]], TOMScalingSamples[[3]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
  xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[2]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[3]),
  main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[2]], scaledTOMSamples[[3]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[2]], TOMScalingSamples[[4]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
  xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[2]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[4]),
  main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[2]], scaledTOMSamples[[4]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

qqUnscaled = qqplot(TOMScalingSamples[[3]], TOMScalingSamples[[4]], plot.it = TRUE, cex = 0.6,
  xlab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[3]), ylab = paste("TOM in", setLabels[4]),
  main = "Q-Q plot of TOM", pch = 20)
# qq plot of the scaled samples
qqScaled = qqplot(scaledTOMSamples[[3]], scaledTOMSamples[[4]], plot.it = FALSE)
points(qqScaled$x, qqScaled$y, col = "red", cex = 0.6, pch = 20)
abline(a=0, b=1, col = "blue")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Unscaled TOM", "Scaled TOM"), pch = 20, col = c("black", "red"))

par(mfrow = c(1,1))  # przywróć domyślny układ
```

In majority of cases scaling improved data congruence significantly.

### Calculation of consensus Topological Overlap

We now calculate the consensus Topological Overlap by taking the component-wise ("parallel") minimum of the
TOMs in individual sets:

```{r consensus TOM}
consensusTOM = pmin(TOM[1,,], TOM[2,,], TOM[3,,], TOM[4,,])
```

Thus, the consensus topological overlap of two genes is only large if the corresponding entries in the four sets are also large.

### Clustering and module identification

We use the consensus TOM as input to hierarchical clustering, and identify modules in the resulting dendrogram
using the Dynamic Tree Cut algorithm [@Langfelder2007].

```{r consensus clustering}
# Clustering
consTree = hclust(as.dist(1-consensusTOM), method = "average")
# We like large modules, so we set the minimum module size relatively high:
minModuleSize = 30
# Module identification using dynamic tree cut:
unmergedLabels = cutreeDynamic(dendro = consTree, 
                               distM = 1-consensusTOM,
                               deepSplit = 2, # The higher the value the more and smaller clusters will be produced. 
                               cutHeight = 0.995,
                               minClusterSize = minModuleSize,
                               pamRespectsDendro = FALSE ) # ? default is TRUE but in manual FALSE is given

unmergedColors = labels2colors(unmergedLabels)

# To see a quick summary of the module detection, we use table(unmergedLabels)
table(unmergedLabels)
```

The Dynamic Tree Cut returned 44 proper modules. The label 0 (grey) is
reserved for genes not assigned to any of the modules. The following code plots the consensus gene dendrogram
together with the preliminary module colors:

```{r consensus dendrogram, fig.width=8, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
plotDendroAndColors(consTree, 
                    unmergedColors, 
                    "Dynamic Tree Cut",
                    dendroLabels = FALSE, 
                    hang = 0.03,
                    addGuide = TRUE, 
                    guideHang = 0.05)
```

### Merging of modules whose expression profiles are very similar

The Dynamic Tree Cut may identify modules whose expression profiles are very similar. It may be prudent
to merge such modules since their genes are highly co-expressed. To quantify co-expression similarity of entire
modules, we calculate their eigengenes (MEs) and cluster them on their consensus correlation, that is the minimum
correlation across the two sets:

```{r consensus module eigengenes, fig.width=7, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
# Calculate module eigengenes
unmergedMEs = multiSetMEs(multiExpr, 
                          colors = NULL, 
                          universalColors = unmergedColors)
# Calculate consensus dissimilarity of consensus module eigengenes
consMEDiss = consensusMEDissimilarity(unmergedMEs)
# Cluster consensus modules
consMETree = hclust(as.dist(consMEDiss), method = "average")
# Plot the result
plot(consMETree, main = "Consensus clustering of consensus module eigengenes",
xlab = "", sub = "")
abline(h=0.25, col = "red")
abline(h=0.3, col = "blue")
```

Some clusters can be merged.

```{r consensus merge modules}
#| output: false
#| message: false
merge = mergeCloseModules(multiExpr, 
                          unmergedLabels, 
                          cutHeight = 0.25, # gives more modules (alternative is 0.3)
                          verbose = 3,
                          corFnc = 'bicor', 
                          corOptions = list(maxPOutliers = 0.05)
                          )

# The variable merge contains various information; we will need the following
# Numeric module labels
moduleLabels = merge$colors
# Convert labels to colors
moduleColors = labels2colors(moduleLabels)
# Eigengenes of the new merged modules:
consMEs = merge$newMEs

save(consensusTOM, moduleColors, file = "../rdata-saved/wgcna/after_merge025.RDa")
```

```{r merged histograms, fig.width=7, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
# multiExpr  - list of expression in sets
# mergedColors - vector of colors after merge (module names after merge)

mergedColors = moduleColors

# 1) Compute module eigengenes for merged modules
mergedMEs <- multiSetMEs(multiExpr, 
                         colors = NULL, 
                         universalColors = mergedColors)

# 2) Compute consensus dissimilarity (1 - MEs correlation)
consMEDiss <- consensusMEDissimilarity(mergedMEs)

# 3) Cluster modules
consMETree <- hclust(as.dist(consMEDiss), method = "average")

# 4) Plot dendrogram
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
plot(consMETree, main = "Consensus clustering of merged consensus module eigengenes",
     xlab = "", sub = "")
```

:::{.callout-note}
How to label modules by color name?
:::

```{r dendro by set, fig.width=8, fig.height=4, dpi=300, dev='png'}
par(mfrow = c(2,2))
plotEigengeneNetworks(consMEs, 
                      setLabels = setLabels, 
                      marDendro = c(0,4,2,0), 
                      plotHeatmaps = FALSE)
```

```{r heat by set, fig.width=7, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
plotEigengeneNetworks(consMEs[[1]]$data, 
                      setLabels = setLabels, 
                      marHeatmap = c(3,4,2,2), 
                      plotDendrograms = FALSE)
```

Lastly, we plot the gene dendrogram again, this time with both the unmerged and the merged module colors:

```{r consensus dendrogram merged, fig.width=9, fig.height=6, dpi=300, dev='png'}
plotDendroAndColors(consTree, 
                    cbind(unmergedColors, moduleColors),
                    c("Unmerged", "Merged"),
                    dendroLabels = FALSE, 
                    hang = 0.03,
                    addGuide = TRUE, 
                    guideHang = 0.05)
```

```{r merge stat}
table(mergedColors)
```


```{r save image}
# saving results up to module merging
rm(list = c('consensusTOM', 'TOM', 'adjacencies')) # these files are already saved elsewhere
save.image('../rdata-saved/wgcna/cons13merged025.RDa')
```

We now save the information necessary in the subsequent parts of the tutorial:

```{r save data}
save(consMEs, moduleColors, moduleLabels, consTree, file = "../rdata-saved/wgcna/Consensus-NetworkConstruction-ctrl-man13mrg025.RData")
```
